title: Imaginarity Resource Theory of Gaussian Quantum Channels
authors: Ting Zhang, Jinchuan Hou, Xiaofei Qi
abstract: Complex numbers play an indispensable role in quantum mechanics and quantum information, as validated by both theoretical analysis and experimental verification. Since quantum information processing inherently relies on quantum channels, the resource theory for quantum channels is equally fundamental to that for quantum states. In this paper, we propose two frameworks for quantifying the imaginarity of Gaussian channels. The first framework regards all real superchannels as free superchannels. Within this setting, we introduce two concrete imaginarity measures for Gaussian channels: I_s^GC based on existing imaginarity measures of Gaussian states, and I_d^GC derived directly from the intrinsic parameters of Gaussian channels, which enjoys high computational simplicity. The second framework adopts only a proper subset of real superchannels as free superchannels. Under this framework, we put forward another imaginarity measure I_c^GC , which is fully determined by the inherent parameters of Gaussian channels and features continuity as well as tractable computation. As a practical application, we employ I_c^GC to investigate the dynamical behavior of Quantum Brownian Motion Gaussian channels throughout the entire evolutionary process.
summary: This paper establishes a resource theory to quantify the 'imaginarity' of Gaussian quantum channels. It introduces three new, computationally efficient measures and applies them to study how the presence of complex numbers evolves in a Quantum Brownian Motion model. The results show that imaginarity can oscillate and decay depending on the temperature and coupling strength of the environment.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a comprehensive resource theory for quantifying the 'imaginarity' (the presence of complex numbers) of Gaussian quantum channels in continuous-variable systems.
main_result: The authors propose two frameworks and three distinct, computable measures of imaginarity ($I_{GC}^s, I_{GC}^d, I_{GC}^c$) that satisfy the requirements of faithfulness and monotonicity, and demonstrate their application to Quantum Brownian Motion.
method: The study uses the mathematical framework of Gaussian superchannels, Heisenberg-Weyl algebra, and matrix representations of channel parameters to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for realness and to construct new resource measures.
model_or_system: The primary models are $n$-mode bosonic Gaussian channels and Gaussian superchannels, with a specific application to a one-mode Quantum Brownian Motion (QBM) model interacting with an Ohmic reservoir.
key_observables: Imaginarity measures ($I_{GC}^s, I_{GC}^d, I_{GC}^c$) and the dynamical evolution of these measures over time.
important_parameters: Channel parameters ($T, N, d$), dimensionless time ($	au$), coupling strength ($\alpha$), non-Markovianity parameter ($x = \omega_c/\omega_0$), and temperature regimes (high vs. low temperature).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the standard matrix representation of Gaussian superchannels and focuses on the weak oscillator-bath coupling regime for the QBM application.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the periodic, decaying oscillations of the $I_{GC}^c$ measure in a high-temperature reservoir for various $\alpha$ and $x$ values; Figure 2 shows similar oscillations in a low-temperature reservoir that asymptotically approach zero.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of imaginarity in Gaussian channels, establishes two resource theory frameworks, defines and proves the validity of three new measures, and concludes with a dynamical study of the QBM Gaussian channel.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a new way to quantify a fundamental aspect of quantum mechanics (complex numbers) through the lens of resource theory and explores its dynamics in dissipative environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Behavior of IGC(ϕ(τ)) as a function of the dimensionless time τ = ωct in the high-temperature Ohmic reservoir with kBT/ℏωc = 100. (a) IGC(ϕ(τ)) versus τ for fixed x = ωc

FIG. 2: Behavior of IGC(ϕ(τ)) as a function of the dimensionless time τ = ωct in a low-temperature Ohmic reservoir with kBT/ℏωc = 10. (a) Plots of IGC(ϕ(τ)) as a function of the parameter τ for fixed x = ωc
